task_completion.description: Input the given physics parameters into GeoGebra.
task_completion.visual_evidence: GeoGebra algebra view shows variables defined for mass (0.145), Cd (0.30), Area (0.0043), air density (1.225), initial velocity (44.7), launch angle (30 degrees or equivalent in radians), and gravity (9.8).

task_completion.description: Model the baseball trajectory in a vacuum.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A parabolic curve is plotted in the graphics view, and/or parametric equations for the standard projectile motion (without drag) are visible in the algebra view.

task_completion.description: Model the baseball trajectory in air with drag.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A second curve (typically shorter and with a steeper descent) is plotted in the graphics view, and/or equations, vectors, or numerical integration commands (e.g., NIntegral, ODE solvers) incorporating the drag force are visible in the algebra view.

task_completion.description: Identify the landing points (y=0) for both trajectories.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Points are explicitly marked on the x-axis (y=0) where the curves intersect, or the x-intercept values are calculated and displayed in the algebra view (e.g., a value around 176.6 for the vacuum trajectory).

task_completion.description: Save the simulation as baseball_physics.ggb in ~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A save dialog showing the filename 'baseball_physics.ggb' and the directory path '~/Documents/GeoGebra/projects/', or a terminal/file explorer window confirming the file was saved in the correct location.

integrity.description: Verify the agent interacted with the GeoGebra GUI to build the simulation.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show the GeoGebra application open with active user inputs in the algebra and graphics views, rather than a terminal script blindly generating a .ggb file.

integrity.description: Verify the trajectories and landing points are genuinely computed by the software.
integrity.visual_evidence: The curves and landing points are defined by mathematical relationships, functions, or commands in the algebra view, not just hardcoded as static, disconnected points placed manually on the canvas.

integrity.description: Verify the agent didn't just type the expected answers as plain text.
integrity.visual_evidence: There are no standalone text boxes or labels just stating the landing points without corresponding geometric or algebraic objects generating those values in the workspace.

privileged_info_for_vlm: Expected filename: baseball_physics.ggb. Expected vacuum trajectory landing point: ~176.6m. Expected drag constant (k = 0.5 * rho * Cd * A / m): 0.00545. Note on drag landing point: A mathematically perfect physics simulation yields a landing point of ~109.8m, while a common independent-axis calculation error yields ~118.2m (the original programmatic verifier expected 115.0m - 135.0m due to this error). The VLM should accept the drag landing point if it aligns with either correct physics or the common error, provided the simulation is genuinely built.
